title: NZ: Site Safety Induction
subtitle: Essential Site Safety for New Zealand Civil Construction
description: Comprehensive site safety induction for workers entering NZ civil construction. Covers HSWA 2015, WorkSafe NZ, NZS standards, CoPTTM traffic management, and key site hazards.

lessons.title: Health and Safety at Work Act 2015
lessons.content:
## Health and Safety at Work Act 2015 (HSWA)

The Health and Safety at Work Act 2015 (HSWA) is New Zealand's primary workplace health and safety legislation. It came into force on 4 April 2016, replacing the Health and Safety in Employment Act 1992 following recommendations from the Royal Commission on the Pike River Coal Mine Tragedy.

## Why HSWA Was Introduced

New Zealand had one of the worst workplace fatality rates in the developed world prior to 2016. The Pike River disaster (29 deaths, November 2010) catalysed comprehensive reform. HSWA introduced a new duty structure, stronger penalties, and a focus on worker engagement.

## PCBUs — Persons Conducting a Business or Undertaking

**PCBU** is the central duty holder under HSWA. Almost every business is a PCBU, including:
- Companies, partnerships, sole traders
- Contractors and subcontractors
- Labour hire companies
- Franchisors

**Primary duty of a PCBU (Section 36):**
A PCBU must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, the health and safety of:
- Workers it employs or engages
- Workers whose activities it influences or directs
- Other persons at risk from its work (visitors, public, neighbouring properties)

## Reasonably Practicable

**Reasonably practicable** is the key qualifier throughout HSWA. It means doing what is reasonably able to be done, weighing:
- The likelihood of the hazard or risk occurring
- The degree of harm that could result
- What the duty holder knows (or ought to know) about the hazard
- Availability and suitability of ways to eliminate or minimise the risk
- After weighing all above: the cost of eliminating or minimising the risk

Cost alone is not a reason to avoid safety measures — it is the last factor considered, not the first.

## Duties of Care Under HSWA

| Duty Holder | Key Obligations |
|-------------|----------------|
| **PCBU** | Primary duty — safe work environment, equipment, systems, information, training, supervision |
| **Officers** (directors, senior managers) | Due diligence — verify PCBU is meeting duties |
| **Workers** | Take reasonable care for own H&S and others affected by their acts/omissions |
| **Other persons** (visitors, contractors) | Take reasonable care for own H&S |

## Officers' Due Diligence

**Officers** (company directors, partners, senior managers with significant influence) have a personal duty to exercise due diligence:
- Acquire and keep up-to-date knowledge of H&S matters
- Understand the nature of the PCBU's operations and associated H&S risks
- Ensure appropriate resources and processes are used
- Verify the PCBU is complying with HSWA

Officers can be prosecuted personally even if the company is also prosecuted.

## Health and Safety Representatives (HSRs)

Workers can request the election of a Health and Safety Representative (HSR):
- HSRs represent the workers in a work group
- Can issue Provisional Improvement Notices (PINs) where they believe a breach of HSWA is occurring
- Can direct work to cease if serious risk to health and safety exists and the situation is urgent
- Protected from disadvantage for exercising HSR functions

## Notifiable Events

PCBUs must notify WorkSafe NZ immediately of notifiable events:

| Event Type | Definition |
|-----------|------------|
| **Notifiable death** | Death of a person due to work-related incident |
| **Notifiable injury/illness** | Serious physical injury requiring immediate hospital treatment; amputation; serious head/eye injury; serious burn; loss of bodily function |
| **Notifiable incident** | Near miss where person could have been killed or seriously harmed |

**Notification requirements:**
- Notify WorkSafe immediately (by fastest means — phone: 0800 030 040)
- Preserve scene until WorkSafe inspector arrives or authorises disturbance
- Keep records of the event

## Penalties Under HSWA

| Offence Category | Individual Penalty | PCBU Penalty |
|-----------------|-------------------|--------------|
| Category 1 (reckless conduct) | Up to $300,000 / 5 years imprisonment | Up to $3,000,000 |
| Category 2 (failure causing death/serious injury) | Up to $150,000 | Up to $1,500,000 |
| Category 3 (failure to comply with duty) | Up to $50,000 | Up to $500,000 |

## Key Takeaways

- HSWA 2015 replaced HSE Act 1992 following Pike River Royal Commission
- PCBU = central duty holder — almost every business qualifies
- 'Reasonably practicable' — weigh likelihood, severity, knowledge, availability and cost (in that order)
- Officers have personal due diligence duties — not just the company
- HSRs can issue PINs and direct work to cease in urgent serious risk situations
- Notify WorkSafe immediately for notifiable events — preserve the scene

lessons.title: WorkSafe NZ — Enforcement and Powers
lessons.content:
## WorkSafe NZ — Regulator and Enforcement

WorkSafe New Zealand is the primary workplace health and safety regulator, established under the WorkSafe New Zealand Act 2013. It is a Crown entity with responsibility for administering and enforcing the Health and Safety at Work Act 2015.

## WorkSafe's Functions

- Monitoring and enforcing compliance with HSWA
- Investigating notifiable events and workplace incidents
- Providing guidance, education, and tools to businesses
- Issuing codes of practice and guidelines
- Engaging with industry sectors on systemic safety issues

## WorkSafe Inspector Powers

WorkSafe inspectors have broad powers under HSWA:

**Entry and inspection:**
- Enter any workplace at any reasonable time without prior notice
- Take photographs, video, samples, or measurements
- Require persons to provide information or answer questions
- Inspect documents and records
- Require production of documents or other items

**Enforcement tools:**

| Tool | Description | Duration |
|------|-------------|----------|
| **Improvement Notice** | Requires remedy of breach within specified timeframe | Inspector sets timeframe |
| **Prohibition Notice** | Prohibits activity until risk is addressed — immediate effect | Until conditions met |
| **Non-disturbance Notice** | Preserves scene after notifiable event | Until investigation complete |
| **Infringement Notice** | On-the-spot fine for minor breaches | N/A — penalty $100–$10,000 |

## Improvement Notices

An inspector issues an Improvement Notice when they believe a HSWA breach is occurring or has occurred:
- States the provisions believed to be breached
- States the reasons
- Requires the breach to be remedied within a specified period
- PCBU must fix the issue within the timeframe
- PCBU may appeal to the District Court within 14 days

## Prohibition Notices

A Prohibition Notice is issued when an inspector believes an activity involves **serious risk** to health and safety:
- Takes effect immediately
- Prohibits the activity until the risk is remedied
- Can apply to an entire site or specific operations
- Failing to comply with a Prohibition Notice is a Category 2 offence
- Can be appealed — but the work stops while appeal is pending unless court orders otherwise

**Civil construction examples when a Prohibition Notice may be issued:**
- Unprotected excavation with workers at the base in unstable soil
- Crane operation within reach of overhead power lines without de-energisation
- Workers without fall protection adjacent to unguarded excavation edges > 1.5 m
- Mobile plant reversing near workers in a zone with no spotter and restricted visibility

## Prosecution

WorkSafe can prosecute PCBUs, officers, and workers for HSWA offences:

**Prosecution process:**
1. Investigation (following notifiable event, complaint, or proactive inspection)
2. Evidence gathering (site inspection, interviews, document review)
3. Decision to prosecute (public interest test, sufficiency of evidence)
4. Filing charges in the District Court
5. Court process — verdict and sentencing

**Defences:**
- Took all reasonably practicable steps to ensure health and safety
- Emergency circumstances beyond control

## WorkSafe Priority Sectors

WorkSafe focuses enforcement resources on high-risk sectors. Construction is consistently a priority sector:
- Approximately **50–70 fatalities per year** in NZ workplaces
- Construction: typically 10–15% of fatalities
- Agriculture, forestry, and transport are also priority sectors

## Industry Compliance Strategy

WorkSafe uses a compliance and enforcement model:
1. **Education and awareness**: Guidance, toolkits, sector engagement
2. **Influence and support**: Work with businesses to lift standards
3. **Regulate**: Monitor and enforce — inspections, notices
4. **Enforce**: Prosecution for serious breaches

The vast majority of contact with WorkSafe is educational — prosecution is reserved for the most serious cases.

## Key Takeaways

- WorkSafe NZ is the primary regulator — established 2013, enforces HSWA
- Inspectors can enter without notice, take evidence, and require information
- Improvement Notice: fix breach within timeframe — appealable within 14 days
- Prohibition Notice: immediate stop work — serious risk — must comply immediately
- Prosecution: Category 1 up to $3M PCBU / $300k + 5 years imprisonment individual
- Construction is a WorkSafe priority sector — proactive inspections occur

questions.q: What does PCBU stand for and when does a business qualify as a PCBU?
options: Person Conducting a Business or Undertaking — almost every business qualifies
Principal Construction Business Unit — only large contractors
Public Construction and Building Union — government only
Person Controlling a Building or Undertaking — building owners only
questions.explanation: PCBU = Person Conducting a Business or Undertaking. Almost every business qualifies — companies, sole traders, contractors, subcontractors, labour hire firms, and franchisors.

questions.q: Under HSWA, what must a PCBU consider LAST when assessing reasonably practicable measures?
options: The degree of harm that could result
The cost of eliminating or minimising the risk
questions.explanation: Cost is the last factor — weighed only after considering likelihood, severity, knowledge, and availability of controls. A PCBU cannot avoid safety measures simply because they are expensive.

questions.q: A worker's arm is amputated in a civil construction incident. What must the PCBU do immediately?
options: File a written report with WorkSafe within 48 hours
Notify WorkSafe immediately by fastest means (phone 0800 030 040) and preserve the scene
Complete an ACC claim form and notify WorkSafe within 7 days
Notify the site supervisor only — WorkSafe notification required only for fatalities
questions.explanation: Amputation is a notifiable injury under HSWA. The PCBU must notify WorkSafe IMMEDIATELY (phone 0800 030 040) and preserve the scene until WorkSafe authorises disturbance.

questions.q: A WorkSafe inspector arrives on site and issues a Prohibition Notice. What must the PCBU do?
options: Continue work while appealing within 30 days
Stop the prohibited activity immediately — the notice takes effect immediately
Stop work only if a court confirms the notice within 24 hours
questions.explanation: Prohibition Notices take effect immediately — the prohibited activity must stop at once. Failure to comply is a Category 2 offence. While the notice can be appealed, work must stop while the appeal is pending unless a court orders otherwise.

questions.q: What is the maximum penalty for a PCBU convicted of a Category 1 HSWA offence (reckless conduct)?
questions.explanation: Category 1 (reckless conduct) carries penalties up to $3,000,000 for a PCBU and $300,000 / 5 years imprisonment for individuals. These are the highest HSWA penalties.

lessons.title: Hazard Management
lessons.content:
## Hazard Management in Civil Construction

Systematic hazard management is the operational core of any effective safety management system. Under HSWA, PCBUs must identify, assess, and control hazards as far as reasonably practicable.

## Step 1: Hazard Identification

Hazards must be identified before work begins — and continuously during the work:

**Identification methods:**
- **Pre-start inspections**: Daily plant and site walk before work commences
- **Site Safety and Health Plan (SSSP)**: Prepared before mobilisation for all notifiable work
- **Task-specific Job Safety Analysis (JSA)**: Step-by-step breakdown of a specific task
- **Toolbox talks**: Team discussion of daily hazards
- **Near miss reporting**: Workers report close calls — these are leading indicators
- **Change management review**: Any change in scope, equipment, or method triggers new hazard identification

**Types of hazards on civil construction sites:**

| Category | Examples |
|----------|---------|
| Physical | Falls from height, struck-by from plant, excavation collapse |
| Mechanical | Pinch points, rotating parts, hydraulic failure |
| Environmental | Noise, vibration, UV radiation, temperature extremes |
| Chemical | Diesel exhaust, bitumen fumes, hydraulic fluid, solvents |
| Ergonomic | Manual handling, awkward posture, repetitive strain |
| Psychosocial | Fatigue, stress, bullying, remote work isolation |

## Step 2: Risk Assessment

**Risk = Likelihood x Severity**

| Risk Level | Likelihood | Severity | Response |
|-----------|-----------|---------|---------|
| Extreme | Likely | Fatality | Stop work — redesign required |
| High | Possible | Serious injury | Urgent controls — site manager sign-off |
| Medium | Unlikely | Moderate injury | Documented controls |
| Low | Rare | Minor injury | Standard precautions |

Risk assessments must be:
- Documented (SSSP hazard register)
- Reviewed when conditions change
- Communicated to all workers on site

## Step 3: Control Hierarchy

HSWA requires applying controls in priority order (highest effectiveness first):

1. **Elimination**: Remove the hazard entirely
   - Redesign the task to remove the hazardous element
   - Use pre-fabricated components to reduce on-site cutting
2. **Substitution**: Replace with a lower risk option
   - Replace solvent-based product with water-based alternative
   - Use an excavator to move heavy material instead of manual lifting
3. **Isolation**: Separate the hazard from workers
   - Exclusion zones around plant and machinery
   - Barrier systems at excavation edges
4. **Engineering controls**: Physical risk reduction
   - Guarding on machinery
   - Noise enclosures
   - Trench shoring systems
5. **Administrative controls**: Procedures and work practices
   - Safe work procedures, permits to work
   - Supervision, training, job rotation
   - Toolbox talks communicating specific hazards
6. **PPE**: Last resort — protects the individual
   - Must be used WITH higher-level controls for significant risks
   - Must fit properly and be maintained

## Site-Specific Safety Plan (SSSP)

An SSSP is required for all **notifiable work** under HSWA regulations. Notifiable work in construction includes:
- Excavations deeper than 1.5 m
- Work on or near live services (electrical, gas, water, telecom)
- Work at height > 5 m
- Use of explosive-powered tools
- Work on or near pressurised systems

**SSSP must include:**
- Description of the notifiable work
- Hazard identification for all activities
- Specific controls for each hazard
- Emergency procedures
- Worker consultation records
- Names of responsible persons

The SSSP must be accessible to all workers on site. It must be reviewed and updated when conditions change.

## Key Takeaways

- Identify hazards daily — pre-starts, JSAs, toolbox talks, near miss reports
- Assess risk: Likelihood x Severity = Risk Level
- Apply control hierarchy: Elimination first — PPE last
- SSSP mandatory for all notifiable work — kept current, accessible to all workers
- Near misses are gold — report them, investigate them, prevent the next event

lessons.title: PPE Requirements
lessons.content:
## Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) Requirements

PPE is the last line of defence in the hierarchy of controls — it does not eliminate or reduce the hazard, it only protects the individual if other controls fail. On civil construction sites, standard PPE is mandatory at all times, with additional specialised PPE for specific tasks.

## Standard Site PPE — Non-Negotiable

The following PPE is required on all civil construction sites in New Zealand:

### Hard Hat (Safety Helmet)
- **Standard**: AS/NZS 1801 Class A or C
- **Purpose**: Protection from falling objects, overhead hazards, and head strikes
- **Maintenance**: Replace after any significant impact (even if no visible damage), every 3–5 years, or as per manufacturer
- **Fit**: Must fit correctly — no backwards wearing (unless chin strap forward-designed)
- Do NOT paint, drill, or apply stickers to hard hats — compromises structural integrity

### High-Visibility Vest or Clothing
- **Standard**: AS/NZS 4602.1 Class D or equivalent
- **Purpose**: Visibility to plant operators and road users
- **Minimum**: Fluorescent vest or t-shirt with retroreflective tape
- All workers and visitors within 10 m of active plant or traffic must wear hi-vis
- Dirty or damaged hi-vis must be replaced — reflective tape effectiveness reduces with soil/grease contamination

### Safety Footwear
- **Standard**: AS/NZS 2210.3 — steel capped boots
- **Purpose**: Crush protection (200J toe cap), puncture resistance, slip resistance
- **Sole requirement**: Steel mid-sole protection recommended for sites with rebar, sharp aggregate, or nail hazards
- Must cover the ankle — no runners, sandals, or shoes without steel cap

### Eye Protection
- **Standard**: AS/NZS 1337
- **Required for**: Grinding, cutting, drilling, concrete work, chemical handling, chipping
- **Types**: Safety glasses (splash/chip), goggles (chemical/grinding dust), face shield (grinding, bitumen spray)
- Prescription glasses alone do NOT meet the standard — safety glasses worn over or prescription safety glasses required

### Hearing Protection
- **Required when**: Noise exceeds 85 dB(A) for 8-hour exposure (WorkSafe NZ trigger level)
- **Types**: Earplugs (foam or reusable, NRR rated), earmuffs (over-ear, higher NRR)
- **Common noisy tasks**: Jack hammering, compactor operation, grinder use, plate compactor
- Hearing loss is permanent and cumulative — damage from years of exposure is irreversible

### Gloves
- Task-specific: general-purpose leather gloves, chemical-resistant nitrile, cut-resistant
- **Do NOT wear gloves** near rotating machinery (drill presses, lathes) — gloves can be caught in rotating parts
- Chemical gloves must be rated for the specific substance being handled

## Task-Specific PPE

| Task | Additional PPE Required |
|------|------------------------|
| Working at height | Full-body harness, shock absorber, lanyard, anchor |
| Concrete and cement work | Waterproof gloves, rubber boots, eye protection (alkali burns) |
| Chipseal and bitumen | Heat-resistant gloves, face shield, chemical apron |
| Grinding and cutting | Face shield over safety glasses, hearing protection |
| Chemical handling | Chemical-rated gloves, goggles, apron |
| Confined space | Respiratory protection, SCBA if required |
| Chainsaw operation | Chainsaw chaps, visor, cut-resistant gloves |

## PPE Management Responsibilities

**PCBU must:**
- Provide appropriate PPE at no cost to the worker
- Ensure PPE is correctly fitted to each worker
- Provide training on correct use, maintenance, and storage
- Replace worn or damaged PPE promptly
- Keep records of PPE provision

**Workers must:**
- Wear PPE as instructed
- Inspect PPE before use
- Report damaged or defective PPE immediately
- Not modify or remove PPE without authorisation
- Store PPE correctly to maintain effectiveness

## PPE is NOT a Substitute for Higher-Level Controls

A common error is relying on PPE instead of engineering or administrative controls:
- Providing hi-vis vests does NOT replace traffic management
- Providing hearing protection does NOT replace noise engineering controls
- Providing safety glasses does NOT replace machine guarding
PPE must supplement — not replace — higher-level controls for significant hazards.

## Key Takeaways

- Standard site PPE: hard hat (AS/NZS 1801), hi-vis (AS/NZS 4602.1), steel boots (AS/NZS 2210.3)
- Hearing protection required above 85 dB(A) — hearing loss is permanent
- PCBU provides PPE at no cost, properly fitted, with training
- PPE is the last resort in the control hierarchy — must not replace higher-level controls
- Task-specific PPE required for grinding, chemicals, working at height, concrete work

questions.q: In the HSWA control hierarchy, which control is most effective and should be applied first?
questions.explanation: Elimination — removing the hazard entirely — is the most effective control and should always be explored first. PPE is the least effective and is the last resort.

questions.q: What NZ standard applies to hi-visibility clothing on civil construction sites?
questions.explanation: AS/NZS 4602.1 Class D applies to high-visibility safety garments for day and night use. AS/NZS 1801 is for hard hats, AS/NZS 2210.3 for safety footwear, AS/NZS 1337 for eye protection.

questions.q: At what noise level (8-hour exposure) does WorkSafe NZ require hearing protection?
questions.explanation: WorkSafe NZ trigger level for mandatory hearing protection is 85 dB(A) for an 8-hour exposure. Hearing damage from noise is permanent and cumulative — cannot be reversed.

questions.q: Who is responsible for providing PPE to workers on a civil construction site?
options: The worker — they must supply their own PPE
The PCBU — at no cost to the worker
WorkSafe NZ — through a PPE scheme
The main contractor only if workers are directly employed
questions.explanation: Under HSWA, the PCBU must provide appropriate PPE at no cost to workers, ensure correct fit, provide training, and replace worn or damaged PPE promptly.

lessons.title: CoPTTM Basics
lessons.content:
## Code of Practice for Temporary Traffic Management (CoPTTM)

CoPTTM is the New Zealand standard for managing road safety around roadworks. Published by Waka Kotahi NZTA, it defines the requirements for planning, implementing, and operating temporary traffic management (TTM) on public roads.

## Why CoPTTM Exists

Roadworks create significant hazards for:
- **Workers** on and near the road (struck-by risk from vehicles)
- **Drivers** navigating around work zones (crash risk from unexpected conditions)
- **Pedestrians and cyclists** diverted through unfamiliar paths

Without consistent, properly implemented traffic management, road workers face one of the highest struck-by risks in the construction industry.

## CoPTTM Qualification Requirements

| Role | CoPTTM Qualification | Description |
|------|---------------------|-------------|
| **Temporary Traffic Controller (TTC)** | TCP (Traffic Control Person) | Controls traffic flow through work zone using stop/slow bat |
| **Site Traffic Management Supervisor (STMS)** | STMS Level 1 or 2 | Plans and supervises all TTM on site |
| **Traffic Management Designer** | STMS Level 2 or specialist | Designs complex TTM plans |

**TCP (formerly Traffic Control Person):**
- Minimum training: 2-day CoPTTM TCP course + assessment
- Scope: Operating stop/slow bat to control traffic on low-complexity worksites

**STMS (Site Traffic Management Supervisor):**
- Level 1: Low-complexity urban and rural roads
- Level 2: High-speed roads, motorways, complex intersections
- Responsibilities: Prepare TMP, implement TTM, supervise TCPs, liaise with road controlling authority

## Traffic Management Plan (TMP)

A TMP must be prepared for all works affecting public roads:
- Prepared or approved by a qualified STMS
- Shows layout of signs, cones, barriers, and vehicle paths
- References standard CoPTTM layouts (where applicable)
- Submitted to and approved by the road controlling authority (RCA) before work commences
- Must be site-specific — copy-paste generic TMPs are not acceptable

**TMP contents:**
- Site location and description
- Type of work and duration
- Speed environment (posted vs temporary speed limit)
- Pedestrian and cyclist management
- Signs, cones, barriers, and delineation layout
- TCP/STMS on-site hours

## Underground Services — 0800 DIG KNOW

Before any excavation near roads, all underground services must be identified:

**0800 DIG KNOW** (now beforeUdig.co.nz):
- Request utility maps from all service owners
- Minimum 3 working days before excavation
- Identify electricity, gas, water, wastewater, telecom, and irrigation
- Use cable locator to verify positions (maps are indicative only)
- Hand dig within 500 mm of identified service locations

**Safe digging zone near services:**
- Electric (high voltage): 1.0 m hand dig zone each side
- Electric (low voltage): 500 mm hand dig zone
- Gas (distribution): 500 mm hand dig zone
- Telecom/data: 250 mm hand dig zone

## CoPTTM Speed Management

CoPTTM prescribes temporary speed limits for work zones:
- **30 km/h**: Workers within 3 m of live traffic lane, urban areas
- **45 km/h**: Workers within 5 m of live lane, rural areas with adequate sight distance
- **Variable speed limit signs**: Used on high-speed roads to step speeds down

Temporary speed limits must be enforced consistently — workers should not allow drivers to exceed the temporary limit without taking action (call Police if required).

## Key Takeaways

- CoPTTM: NZ standard for temporary traffic management — Waka Kotahi NZTA
- TCP: controls traffic; STMS: plans, supervises, prepares TMP
- TMP approved by RCA before work starts — no copy-paste generic plans
- 0800 DIG KNOW / beforeUdig: request service plans 3 days before excavation
- Hand dig within 500 mm of identified services — never excavate blind near utilities

questions.q: What NZ document governs temporary traffic management on public roads?
options: CoPTTM (Code of Practice for Temporary Traffic Management)
questions.explanation: CoPTTM (Code of Practice for Temporary Traffic Management) published by Waka Kotahi NZTA governs all TTM on public roads in New Zealand.

questions.q: Who is responsible for preparing the Traffic Management Plan (TMP) on a NZ road project?
options: A qualified STMS (Site Traffic Management Supervisor)
questions.explanation: TMPs must be prepared or approved by a qualified STMS. STMS Level 1 for standard roads, Level 2 for high-speed and complex sites.

questions.q: What is the minimum advance notice required when requesting utility plans before excavation via beforeUdig.co.nz?
questions.explanation: Minimum 3 working days notice is required to request utility maps before excavation. This allows all service owners time to respond with their records.

questions.q: What is the minimum hand-dig zone width either side of an identified low voltage electricity cable?
questions.explanation: 500 mm hand dig zone is required either side of identified low voltage electricity cables. Mechanical excavation within this zone risks cable strike — electrocution hazard.

questions.q: What does STMS stand for in NZ traffic management?
questions.explanation: STMS = Site Traffic Management Supervisor. An STMS is a qualified person who designs and implements Traffic Management Plans (TMPs) on NZ roads. STMS qualification is required to supervise traffic management on roads with speed limits above 70 km/h.

questions.q: Who do you call before excavating near underground services in New Zealand?
options: 0800 DIG KNOW (the national utility locate service)
questions.explanation: 0800 DIG KNOW (0800 344 566) is New Zealand's national utility locate service. Before ANY excavation near services, call 0800 DIG KNOW to identify underground utilities and obtain locate information.

lessons.title: Incident Response and Notifiable Events
lessons.content:
## Emergency and Incident Response on Civil Construction Sites

Every civil construction site must have a documented emergency response plan. Emergencies are unpredictable — when they occur, the first 2–3 minutes determine outcomes. Pre-planned, rehearsed response is the difference between a managed event and a catastrophe.

## Site Emergency Plan Requirements

**The emergency plan must cover:**
- Emergency contacts (111, WorkSafe 0800 030 040, site manager, site address/GPS for emergency services)
- Location of first aid kit, AED, fire extinguishers, spill kits
- Designated muster point(s)
- Emergency warden / first aider contact details
- Evacuation routes for site
- Procedures for: medical emergency, fire, buried worker, chemical spill, vehicle rollover

**First aid requirements (NZ):**
- Minimum one trained first aider must be on site at all times during work hours
- First aid training: Provide First Aid (Unit Standard 6402) — valid 3 years
- First aid kit: Stocked to NZS 8141:2001 standard, inspected monthly

## Responding to an Incident — DRSABCD

The standard NZ first aid response sequence:

| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| **D** | Danger — assess for danger to yourself, others, and the casualty |
| **R** | Response — check if casualty is conscious |
| **S** | Send for help — call 111 immediately |
| **A** | Airway — open and clear the airway |
| **B** | Breathing — check for normal breathing |
| **C** | CPR — begin 30:2 if not breathing normally |
| **D** | Defibrillation — use AED as soon as available |

**Key principle**: Do not move a casualty with a suspected spinal injury unless in immediate danger (e.g., from fire or further collapse).

## Notifiable Events — Definition and Obligations

Under HSWA, specific events must be notified to WorkSafe immediately:

**Notifiable Death:**
- Death of any person arising from a work-related incident
- Including contractors, subcontractors, visitors, and members of the public

**Notifiable Injury or Illness:**
- Immediate admission to hospital for treatment (not just observation)
- Amputation of any body part
- Serious head or eye injury
- Serious burn (burns to large body surface areas or full thickness)
- Loss of a bodily function (e.g., paralysis, loss of sight)
- Serious laceration requiring surgery

**Notifiable Incident (near miss):**
- Collapse, failure, or malfunction of equipment that could have caused death/serious injury
- Unintended explosion, fire, or flood
- Escape of substances likely to cause serious illness
- Collapse or failure of an excavation exceeding 1.5 m depth
- Collapse or partial collapse of a structure
- Contact with a live electrical service

## Reporting to WorkSafe

**Immediate notification process:**
1. Call WorkSafe NZ: **0800 030 040** (24/7)
2. Provide: Site location, nature of event, injuries, hazards still present
3. **Preserve the scene**: Do not disturb until WorkSafe authorises
   - Exception: Access to provide first aid and save life
   - Exception: Prevent further serious injury
   - Exception: Direction from WorkSafe inspector
4. Keep all records related to the incident
5. Provide written notice within 7 days if required by WorkSafe

## Investigation Requirements

All notifiable events must be investigated:
- **Purpose**: Identify root causes — not to assign blame
- **Team**: Include a worker representative (HSR or JHSC equivalent)
- **Methods**: Site inspection, interviews, document review, timeline reconstruction
- **Root cause analysis**: 5 Whys, bow-tie analysis, fishbone diagram
- **Corrective actions**: Implemented, tracked, and verified for effectiveness
- **Learnings shared**: Site-wide and across the organisation

**Preserve investigation integrity:**
- Photograph scene before any disturbance
- Record witness statements as soon as possible
- Retain broken equipment, failed components as evidence
- Document exact timeline of events

## Key Takeaways

- Emergency plan: 111, 0800 030 040, site address, first aid locations, muster point
- DRSABCD: Danger, Response, Send for help, Airway, Breathing, CPR, Defibrillation
- Notifiable events: deaths, serious injuries, and near-miss incidents — notify WorkSafe IMMEDIATELY
- Preserve the scene after notifiable events — only disturb for first aid or preventing further harm
- Investigate to find root causes — share learnings to prevent recurrence

questions.q: What is the WorkSafe NZ 24/7 notification phone number for notifiable events?
questions.explanation: WorkSafe NZ must be notified of notifiable events on 0800 030 040, available 24/7. This is separate from the emergency 111 call, which should also be made for life-threatening incidents.

questions.q: A large excavation wall collapses but no one is injured. Does this need to be reported to WorkSafe?
options: No — no injuries means no reporting required
Yes — collapse of an excavation exceeding 1.5 m is a notifiable incident even without injuries
Only if the excavation was deeper than 3 m
Only if there was property damage over $10,000
questions.explanation: Collapse or failure of an excavation exceeding 1.5 m depth is a notifiable incident under HSWA regardless of whether injuries occurred. Near-miss events of this severity must be reported.

questions.q: After a notifiable event, the scene should be:
options: Cleaned up immediately to make the site safe for other workers
Preserved and not disturbed until WorkSafe authorises — except for first aid and preventing further harm
Photographed and then cleared within 2 hours
Cleared once the injured worker has been transported to hospital
questions.explanation: HSWA requires scene preservation after notifiable events. Only disturb the scene to provide first aid, prevent further harm, or on direction from WorkSafe. This protects the integrity of the investigation.

questions.q: In the DRSABCD first aid sequence, what does the second 'D' stand for?
questions.explanation: The second D in DRSABCD stands for Defibrillation — use an AED (automated external defibrillator) as soon as it is available. Early defibrillation dramatically improves survival rates in cardiac arrest.

questions.q: Under HSWA 2015, how quickly must WorkSafe NZ be notified after a notifiable event?
questions.explanation: HSWA requires IMMEDIATE notification of notifiable events by the fastest means practicable (typically phone: 0800 030 040). Written notification must follow but the immediate oral notification is the critical first step.

questions.q: A worker witnesses a near miss where an excavator nearly struck a worker. Is this a notifiable incident?
options: No — nobody was hurt so it does not need to be reported
Yes — a notifiable incident includes situations where a person could have been killed or seriously harmed
Only if the operator witnessed it too
Only if the near miss is reported within 1 hour
questions.explanation: A notifiable incident under HSWA includes any unplanned or uncontrolled incident where a person could have been killed or seriously harmed — even if no injury occurred. Near misses must be notified to WorkSafe NZ.
